content:
2 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

When I was a student, I believed one thing:
The more courses I completed, the better my chances of getting hired.
So every time I came across a new course, I enrolled.
Python. SQL. Machine Learning. Deep Learning. Power BI. Tableau. Cloud. And later, every new AI course that started trending.
I genuinely thought I was doing the right thing.
With every certificate I earned, I felt like I was getting one step closer to becoming a great Data Scientist.
But after a while, I noticed something.
I had completed plenty of courses.
Yet when someone asked me to solve a real problem from scratch, I wasn’t as confident as I expected to be.
That’s when I realized…
I wasn’t learning. I was collecting courses.
The mistake I made
I kept asking,
“What’s the next course I should take?”
Instead of asking,
“Can I build something with what I’ve already learned?”
I spent hours watching videos.
Taking notes.
Completing quizzes.
Collecting certificates.
But very little time actually building projects, making mistakes, debugging code, or figuring things out on my own.
What changed everything
As I moved into the industry, I realized something that no course had taught me.
At work, nobody asks,
“How many courses have you completed?”
They ask,
“Can you solve this problem?”
That question changed my entire approach to learning.
How I learn today
Now, I rarely take a course just because it’s trending.
I first identify a problem I want to solve.
If I want to improve my SQL, I work on real datasets.
If I want to learn Machine Learning, I build an end-to-end project.
If I want to understand Generative AI, I create something useful with it.
Courses have become a starting point — not the finish line.
A lesson I wish I had learned as a student
If you’re a student, don’t feel pressured to complete every course you see online.
Finish one.
Apply it.
Build something.
Break it.
Fix it.
Repeat.
One well-executed project will teach you far more than five unfinished courses.
My learning rule today
For every hour I spend learning something new, I make sure I spend even more time applying it.
Because certificates may help your resume get noticed.
But projects, problem-solving, and practical experience are what build confidence — and that’s what interviewers and employers remember.
Looking back, I don’t regret taking those courses.
They gave me direction.
But I do wish I had spent less time chasing certificates and more time creating.
If you’re currently a student or an aspiring Data Scientist, here’s my advice:
Don’t try to learn everything.
Learn what matters.
Apply it.
Then move on to the next challenge.
That’s where real growth happens.
With Appreciation,
Thanusree Gajendran
